data.title: Almost 2 in 3 feel dissatisfied or upset with Trump ballroom, Reflecting Pool, arch projects: Survey
data.description:
A majority of Americans don’t approve of President Trump’s renovation efforts in the U.S.’s capital city, according to a new survey. 

The president has undertaken a series of controversial beautification projects around Washington, D.C., including updating the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, replacing the East Wing of the White House with a multi-million-dollar ballroom and constructing a massive replica of Paris’ Arc de Triomphe near the National Mall. 

A new Washington Post-Ipsos poll asked respondents how they felt about this trio of projects. Nearly 2 in 3 respondents — or 65 percent of those surveyed — reacted negatively, with 27 percent indicating dissatisfaction and 38 percent saying they were “upset” by these efforts. 

Among those with positive reactions, 12 percent said they were “enthusiastic” and 19 percent said they were “satisfied” with these projects.

The poll found a partisan split on this issue. Nearly 7 in 10 of Republican respondents expressed a positive view of these renovations, while about 9 in 10 of the survey’s Democratic respondents expressed a negative view. 

Additionally, the poll asked respondents about Trump’s motivations for these projects. Twenty-six percent said they believed he was committed to making the capital city “more beautiful,” while 51 percent said they thought the president had a “different goal.”

When the latter group was asked what they believed this alternate objective was, 42 percent said to “glorify himself” and 22 percent said to “leave his mark” on D.C.

The survey’s researchers noted that these results do not reveal discrepancies between perceptions of individual projects but instead group all three together. Previous polling has indicated that the public is more widely opposed to the ballroom project than the arch or Reflecting Pool. 

The proposed ballroom has raised concerns over its cost to taxpayers after the project’s price tag was raised from an initial $400 million to a reported $600 million earlier this year. Trump has defended the multi-million-dollar project, saying it would be covered entirely by private donations. 

However, records reported by the Post in June showed that only $293 million of the total cost would be covered by “private sources,” while the rest would come from taxpayer-funded department budgets. 

The Reflecting Pool project has also faced a wave of criticism over the peeling of its newly-installed “American flag blue” base and an algae outbreak shortly after the renovation’s completion. Crews drained the pool again after Independence Day to address these issues. 

The Washington Post-Ipsos poll surveyed over 2,600 U.S. adults from July 8-13 with a margin of error of approximately 1.9 percentage points.

data.title: Man who pleaded guilty to killing Minnesota Democrat sentenced to life in prison
data.description:
The man accused of killing former Minnesota House Speaker Melissa Horton was sentenced to two consecutive life terms, plus 40 years in prison on Thursday.

Vance Boelter, 59, was sentenced to spend the rest of his life behind bars over a year after his fatal shooting of Horton and her husband Mark Horton. To avoid the death penalty, Boelter pleaded guilty in June 2026 to federal charges relating to the killings, critically wounding state Sen. John Hoffman and his wife Yvette Hoffman and two stalking charges.

The Horton’s two children – Sophie and Colin – released a statement on Thursday following the sentence.

“We are grateful for all of the work that has led to the resolution of the federal case,” they wrote. “We appreciate the work to bring justice for all of the victims. Nothing can replace what we have lost, and there are no words that can blunt our pain.”

“Our parents were public figures and known to people, but to us they were just Mom and Dad. And they were the happiest people to just be alive,” the siblings continued. “They were so excited every day—to do the big things and the little things, to be parents, to be friends, to be leaders, and so much more.”

Boelter also faces pending state charges for two counts of murder, four counts of attempted murder, impersonating a police officer and animal cruelty. The Hennepin County Attorney says they are prepared to prosecute him in state court.

Prosecutors in federal court stated that on June 14, 2025, Boelter stalked the target families before driving to their homes. In his homemade police officer disguise, he claimed to be doing a welfare check before opening fire on both families. That night, Boelter also approached two other lawmakers’ residences, but left when the door was not answered.

John and Yvette Hoffman both say they suffer from permanent physical damage due to the violent event, the Associated Press reported. 

“My wife and I now live with injuries. We live with our bodies remembering the gunfire,” John Hoffman said in court on Thursday.

Boelter is scheduled to appear in court for his state charges on Aug. 3.

data.title: Americans see China as more advanced on AI than US: Pew survey
data.description:
A plurality of respondents to a recent survey believe China is further along than the U.S. on developing AI, as Trump administration officials accuse a Chinese startup of stealing intellectual property (IP).

Thirty-six percent of respondents to the Pew Research Center poll think China is more advanced on AI than the U.S., triple the 12 percent who believe it is the other way around. One-third of respondents were unsure, while 18 percent said the two countries are equally advanced. 

Michael Kratsios, who heads the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy, said Wednesday the administration has information that Moonshot AI distilled Anthropic’s Fable model to develop its latest model Kimi K3.

The model, which the Chinese-based firm released last week, threatens the coding leads of U.S. labs and models — such as Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI’s GPT-5.6 Sol.

“To do this they developed a sophisticated internal platform to conduct large scale distillation against U.S. models, allowing them to quickly switch between multiple methods of access to avoid detection,” Kratsios wrote on social platform X.

Distillation is a technique used to transfer the knowledge of a larger, more capable model to a smaller one.

Testing of Kimi K3 by the U.S. Center for AI Standards and Innovation and its U.K. counterpart found that the model performs “significantly” below the leading American frontier models. 

There is no true consensus on how far apart the U.S. and China are on AI, as the industry moves rapidly. However, Moonshot’s development of Kimi K3 shows the gap is small, technology experts told The Hill. 

Lin Jian, a spokesperson for the Chinese Foreign Ministry, said Wednesday the country “opposes politicizing and instrumentalizing trade and tech issues,” amid the U.S. government’s allegations against Moonshot.

“The development of AI in China comes from greater self-reliance and strength in science and technology and is fueled by China’s vision of extensive consultation and joint contribution for shared benefit,” Lin told reporters.

As for whether Americans believe the U.S. should lead on AI, 43 percent of respondents to the Pew survey said it is extremely or very important. Slightly more than one-third said it is somewhat important, while 22 percent said it was either not too or not at all important. 

Pew conducted the poll from June 22-28, interviewing 3,488 U.S. adults. The survey had a margin of error of 1.8 percentage points.

data.title: Tuberville: Mamdani will do something 'stupid' enough to 'send him to Gitmo'
data.description:
Sen. Tommy Tuberville (R-Ala.) went after New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani (D) on Thursday, saying the NYC mayor is going to do something “stupid” enough to get him sent to Tuberville: Mamdani will do something ‘stupid’ enough to ‘send him to Gitmo,’ a nickname for Guantanamo Bay in Cuba.

“For some reason, we have enough people in New York that would vote for this guy to run the largest and the most popular city in the world, just goes to show you how close we are to losing our country,” Tuberville, who won the Republican nomination in the Alabama governor’s race earlier this year, told conservative personality Benny Johnson in an interview while discussing Mamdani.

“And — he will, sooner or later, he will do something more stupid than what he’s done, to where we’ll finally say, ‘Enough’s enough,’ go putting handcuffs on him and get him the hell out of here and send him to Gitmo, because the guy hates this country, he wants to change it and that’s not going to happen,” he added

Guantanamo Bay is most famous for being a military base where terror suspects are held. It gained infamy for torture and abuse accusations during the U.S.’s war on terrorism. 

Earlier this year, Tuberville made a post connecting Mamdani’s Ramadan iftar at City Hall with the Sept. 11, 2001 attacks which received backlash from Democrats. California Gov. Gavin Newsom called the Alabama senator “a racist piece of s—” over the post.

Mamdani, a democratic socialist, stepped into the political spotlight last year with a win in the Democratic primary for New York City mayor over former New York Gov. Andrew Cuomo. Within the last year, he has become one of the most prominent faces in the Democratic Party.

In recent months, democratic socialists have racked up electoral victories in places like New York, Los Angeles, Washington, D.C., and Colorado. 

House Republicans have recently made concerns about a creeping communist threat a key part of their case for the midterms, alleging that Democratic candidates furthest to the left are not just self-described democratic socialists, but are pushing for a society based on collective ownership of resources. 

The Hill has reached out to Mamdani’s press office for comment.

data.title: Democrats urge appeals court not to overturn mifepristone regulations
data.description:
The entire congressional Democratic caucus filed an amicus brief urging a federal appeals court to retain the Food and Drug Administration’s current rules for prescribing abortion pills. 

Forty-seven Democratic senators and 212 House members asked the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 5th Circuit not to reinstate an in-person requirement to obtain mifepristone, which has not been enforced in five years.  

The brief was filed in an ongoing case brought by Louisiana, which sued the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to restrict access to mifepristone. The state objected to a 2023 FDA regulation that allowed mifepristone to be prescribed through telehealth, in pharmacies and through the mail.

“Louisiana’s requested relief has no basis in law, threatens the congressionally mandated evidence-based process for drug-regulatory decisions, and poses a serious health risk to pregnant individuals,” the Democrats wrote. 

A federal trial court denied Louisiana’s request to temporarily reinstate the requirement while the case proceeds. Louisiana appealed, and the 5th Circuit granted Louisiana’s request, disrupting access and creating upheaval for patients, providers, pharmacies, as well as two of the manufacturers of mifepristone.  

The Supreme Court later paused the 5th Circuit’s ruling, allowing people to continue to fill their mifepristone prescriptions by mail and at pharmacies after receiving care through telemedicine while the case is litigated.

The case is now back before the 5th Circuit, which will hold oral arguments in September. 

“Decades after FDA’s initial approval of mifepristone and years after the in person dispensing requirement was eliminated, Louisiana now seeks to re-impose this onerous nationwide restriction on all Americans. Granting such relief would undermine the science-based statutory framework Congress requires and threatens patient access to reproductive health care,” the Democrats wrote. 

They argued Louisiana shouldn’t be able to dictate policy to all other states.  

“Louisiana is free to make its own laws governing its citizens, but should not be permitted to undermine the FDA’s process,” the lawmakers wrote.  

Mifepristone has been used as part of the most common and recommended regimen for medication abortion for more than 25 years.  

In the wake of the Supreme Court’s ruling ending Roe v. Wade, abortion opponents have grown frustrated with the wide availability of mifepristone, which is typically used as part of a two-drug combination for medication abortion.  

Mail-order pharmacies, combined with blue state “shield laws” protecting clinicians from prosecution, have helped women maintain access even as conservative states ban or severely limit access to abortion clinics.    

As a result, abortions have increased despite the end of Roe.

data.title: Trump defends data centers as he expands pledge to make them 'pay their own way'
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday expanded his pledge that aims to have data centers “pay their own way” for the electricity they use, but he took the opportunity to defend data centers at the same time.

Trump announced that entities ranging from utilities to state governments are joining the pledge, which was already signed by major tech companies.

The pledge, which is voluntary, says that data centers should either bring their own power or pay for the power and additional power resources and infrastructure that they use. It also says that they should invest in local communities and, when possible, allow their backup power to be available for communities in emergency situations.

While the pledge serves as an acknowledgement from the White House that data centers’ significant electricity use has the potential to jack up prices for consumers, Trump also praised the centers, saying that communities should get on board with them.

“You have communities that really want the data centers, and frankly, those are the smart communities because it means tremendous numbers of jobs, very little actual disruption,” he said.

He touted the “tremendous amounts of money” that tech companies are investing to build the infrastructure behind the AI boom.

“You have to convince your community how great these things [are],” Trump added. “You can’t fight it. You have to go with it, and you’re going to have all that protection.”

The White House also unveiled a website listing the pledge’s signatories. This included 27 data center developers, 55 utilities and 106 electric cooperatives. Twenty-three Republican governors have also signed the pledge, a White House official confirmed.

While the White House has touted the pledge as a way to reduce the centers’ impacts on consumers, both left-wing and right-wing critics expressed doubts that it will actually be effective in bringing down consumer costs.

“There’s no enforcement …. if you wanted to call somebody out for violating the pledge, what does that even mean?” asked Travis Fisher, director of energy and environmental policy studies at the libertarian Cato Institute.

The pledge’s website seeks to push back on this argument, acknowledging that skeptics have questioned whether it “could be enforced, given that power prices are set by state regulators, electricity buyers, and electricity sellers.”

“The Ratepayer Protection Pledge now has commitments from every one of them — and from the developers who actually build the facilities,” it reads.

Fisher also said that defining which costs belong to data center companies is complicated.

“Even if you agree in principle that you’re going to own all of your costs, that argument about which costs are actually yours versus someone else’s, or which things were built that could benefit you but also help others — that cost allocation fight is not going to get any simpler,” he said. 

He said that it makes sense to expand the pledge to states and utilities because “state commissions and utilities are the ones who set retail rates,” but governors’ power over state utility regulators may be limited. 

Meanwhile, left-wing critics have also slammed the pledge as “toothless.”

“I honestly think that this will result in higher prices because these companies will feel like they did something to get people off their back, and now they don’t have to worry about keeping rates under control,” said Climate Power senior advisor and former Biden White House official Jesse Lee. “All of it is not only toothless but completely vague, so there’s no specifics about what they’re actually supposed to do.”

data.title: DOJ will withdraw subpoenas against New York Times journalists over Air Force One reporting
data.description:
The Justice Department on Thursday said it would withdraw subpoenas issued to several New York Times reporters earlier this month over their reporting on security concerns regarding the new Air Force One jet gifted by Qatar.

The development came during a hearing in a Manhattan courtroom in which Judge Arun Subramanian was weighing whether to grant the outlet’s motion to quash, according to multiple reports.

“The government is prepared to unilaterally withdraw the subpoenas at this time,” Sean Buckley, the chief counsel to U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton, said at the hearing in U.S. District Court, according to the New York Times.

Four Times journalists —Julian E. Barnes, Eric Lipton, Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt— were hit with subpoenas on July 10, seeking to compel their testimony before a federal grand jury.

The Justice Department also sought the reporters’ phone and text messaging records and, in some cases, those of family members, according to the newspaper.

The Trump administration was pushing to identify the confidential government sources who spoke with reporters about the donated jet’s security capabilities, a move critics see as a brazen attempt to chill press freedom and suppress unfavorable coverage. 

“The Subpoenas were issued in a bad faith effort to harass, intimidate, and retaliate against members of the press for reporting on subjects the President finds embarrassing,” lawyers for the newspaper wrote in a court filing.

Prosecutors in the Southern District of New York had initially asked Subramanian to pause enforcement of the subpoenas for two weeks while it pursues additional investigative steps.

Buckley contended in a Tuesday letter to the judge that the First Amendment does not shield reporters from appearing before a grand jury for questioning “as to their knowledge of criminal conduct, even as to the identities of the sources.”

Prosecutors also pushed back on the Times’s argument that proper legal procedures and Justice Department regulations were not followed in issuing the subpoenas, calling the allegations “inaccurate.”

“Without divulging classified or otherwise confidential and sensitive information, the movants are incorrect, for example, in asserting that the investigation lacks proper predication and that no investigative steps were taken prior to issuance of the subpoenas,” Buckley wrote on behalf of Clayton, adding that prosecutors considered “among other things, the underlying national security equities at stake.”

The newspaper reported earlier this month that President Trump flew on the old Air Force One jet when he left the NATO summit in Turkey because of a security precaution related to the war with Iran. 

The journalists also wrote later in the week that the new plane lacked the “defensive countermeasures” of older models, including advanced antimissile capabilities, citing multiple officials who had been briefed on the retrofitting.

The Justice Department previously told The Hill in a statement that the Times reporters were not its targets but “those leaking classified are.”

“We recognize there may always be natural tension there, but we are not going to ignore the law and stop investigating the people who work in the administration and think it’s OK to leak classified information impacting national security,” a spokesperson said.

data.title: Watch live: Trump welcomes Los Angeles Dodgers to White House
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday afternoon will host the Los Angeles Dodgers in the White House Rose Garden.

The 2025 World Series Champions clinched their 9th title last year against the Toronto Blue Jays, nabbing four out of the seven games.

The president congratulated the team after their victory last November, calling them “incredible CHAMPIONS!!!

“A lesser group of men would never have been able to win that game, or game 6, for that matter,” he wrote at the time on Truth Social. “So many stars made it all happen. Also, congratulations to ownership. What a great job they have done. SEE YOU ALL AT THE WHITE HOUSE!!!”

The event is scheduled to begin at 4:30 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Ocasio-Cortez: GOP's communist warnings 'very silly'
data.description:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) on Thursday called Republicans’ warnings about a supposed communist threat are “very silly.”

Ocasio-Cortez laughed when asked if there were concerns over democratic socialism becoming “full-blown communism” during a gaggle with reporters on Capitol Hill.

“I think it’s very silly,” she added. “Just think about how impossible it has been to raise the minimum wage in America in 10, 15, 20 years. We can’t even raise the minimum wage –– do Republicans really think that this stuff is going to happen? No, they don’t.”

Ocasio-Cortez added that Republicans were “trying to scare people because they don’t want us talking about the fact that we all have the right to health care in this country.”

She continued that Republicans “want to call it communist and they want to call everything they don’t like ‘communist.”

“Because if people actually wake up to the fact that their elected officials are screwing them over when they get here, then they’re going to realize they deserve better and that health care, as a right, isn’t crazy,’ she added.

Republicans and some Democrats have fumed over the successes democratic socialist candidates have had in recent victories against incumbent Democrats during the 2026 midterm election season.

Democratic socialist candidates have seen victories in New York, Pennsylvania and Colorado, as well as major cities like Washington, D.C., and Los Angeles. These candidates have campaigned on addressing rising costs and inflation, holding the Trump administration accountable and against Israel’s partnership with the U.S. following the country’s war in Gaza.

President Trump conflating the candidates’ ideology with communism, said last month communism is the biggest threat facing the country, “maybe since our founding.”

House Republicans have repeated to the president’s claim, with some including Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) claiming that “these crazy little mini-Mamdani’s … are a danger to you and your family.”

“The radical left feels they need to hide what their policies are,” House Republican Conference Chair Lisa McClain (R-Mich.) said on Tuesday. “They soften the language. They invent nicer labels. They call themselves ‘democratic socialists,’ almost as though they’re ashamed to tell the American people what they actually believe.”

“Let’s call it what it is,” she added. “It’s communism.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio last week, as he convened with officials from more than 60 countries, called for an “international response” to left-wing political violence. He said the U.S. will have its counterterrorism efforts shift to crack down on what he called “far-left terror.”

data.title: Live updates: House OKs, Senate rejects war powers resolution; DOJ will drop New York Times subpoenas
data.description:
President Trump is threatening to intensify the war with Iran as some Republican lawmakers urge him to “finish the job,” but there is also widespread skepticism on Capitol Hill on both sides of the aisle about whether he can deliver a victory.

The House narrowly approved a Democratic-sponsored war powers resolution with the help of four GOP lawmakers. Meanwhile, Republicans defeated a motion to advance a similar war powers resolution in the Senate. They come as Trump’s support for the Iran conflict is reaching a new low among his base.

The Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen on Thursday attacked two oil tankers in the Red Sea, a ramping up of the conflict since the group announced a blockade of Saudi ships attempting to go through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait earlier this week. 

In a New York courtroom, federal prosecutors agreed to drop subpoenas issued to New York Times reporters over their coverage of the new Air Force One jet, the Associated Press reported.

Dr. Erica Schwartz faced a vote in the Senate Health Committee for her nomination to be director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, but it was postponed due to attendance.

The Energy Department announced Wednesday a nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, and it drew swift backlash. On Thursday morning, Trump added a condition: The Saudis must join the Abraham Accords. Insiders can read more here.

On Thursday afternoon, Trump will greet the 2025 World Series champion Los Angeles Dodgers at the White House.

Follow live below.
